Search results for "llvmlite binding target machine triple emit object file .o example":

Title: Target information - llvmlite - Read the Docs
URL: https://llvmlite.readthedocs.io/en/v0.45.0rc2/user-guide/binding/target-information.html
Content: Minimal use of this module would be to create a TargetMachine for later use in code generation. EXAMPLE: from llvmlite import binding target =

Title: llvmlite.binding.TargetMachine.emit_object() creates incorrect ...
URL: https://github.com/numba/llvmlite/issues/181
Content: ## Navigation Menu. # Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests... # Provide feedback. We read every piece of feedback, and take your input very seriously. # Saved searches. ## Use saved searches to filter your results more quickly. To see all available qualifiers, see our documentation. # llvmlite.binding.TargetMachine.emit\_object() creates incorrect executable type on Windows #181. ## Footer. I was messing around and trying to create an executable using llvmlite on my Windows machine, but I couldn't get it to work. I noticed that even though all of the default settings told me that the executable would be of the COFF format, it was an ELF executable. Here's an example shell run showing the issue:  . This generated file cannot be run by Windows. I'm running Windows 10, the beta that allows Bash on Windows, and Python 3.5 in a Conda Virtual Environment. I'm using the latest llvmlite version available on Conda. ### Type. ## Issue actions. ### Footer navigation.

Title: Allow llvmlite to also link object code · Issue #311 - GitHub
URL: https://github.com/numba/llvmlite/issues/311
Content: ## Navigation Menu. # Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests... # Provide feedback. We read every piece of feedback, and take your input very seriously. # Saved searches. ## Use saved searches to filter your results more quickly. # Allow llvmlite to also link object code #311. ## Footer. it can also read this IR source code and generate a machine code object file using:. But it seems `llvmlite` does not have the functionality to actually link these object files into an executable. One has to do it by hand, e.g. using `gcc`:. `clang -o a.out file.o`. `"/usr/bin/ld" -z relro --hash-style=gnu --eh-frame-hdr -m elf_x86_64 -dynamic-linker /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 -o c /usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/../../../x86_64-linux-gnu/crt1.o /usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/../../../x86_64-linux-gnu/crti.o /usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/crtbegin.o -L/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1 -L/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/../../../x86_64-linux-gnu -L/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu -L/lib/../lib64 -L/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu -L/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/6.1.1/../../.. One does not need the object file for the JIT function (for numba) it seems. As it is now, one can generate object files, but the final step still requires either `gcc` or `clang` to be installed to do the linking. ### Footer navigation.

Title: 8. Kaleidoscope: Compiling to Object Code - LLVM.org
URL: https://llvm.org/docs/tutorial/MyFirstLanguageFrontend/LangImpl08.html
Content: In this tutorial, we’ll target the current machine. To specify the architecture that you want to target, we use a string called a “target triple”. Fortunately, we don’t need to hard-code a target triple to target the current machine. std:: string  Error; auto  Target  =  TargetRegistry:: lookupTarget(TargetTriple,  Error);// Print an error and exit if we couldn't find the requested target.// This generally occurs if we've forgotten to initialise the// TargetRegistry or we have a bogus target triple. class  ForExprAST  :  public  ExprAST  { std:: string  VarName;  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>  Start,  End,  Step,  Body; public:  ForExprAST(const  std:: string  & VarName,  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>  Start,  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>  End,  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>  Step,  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>  Body)  :  VarName(VarName),  Start(std:: move(Start)),  End(std:: move(End)),  Step(std:: move(Step)),  Body(std:: move(Body))  {}  Value  * codegen()  override;};/// VarExprAST - Expression class for var/in class  VarExprAST  :  public  ExprAST  { std:: vector< std:: pair< std:: string,  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>>>  VarNames;  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>  Body; public:  VarExprAST( std:: vector< std:: pair< std:: string,  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>>>  VarNames,  std:: unique_ptr< ExprAST>  Body)  :  VarNames(std:: move(VarNames)),  Body(std:: move(Body))  {}  Value  * codegen()  override;};/// PrototypeAST - This class represents the "prototype" for a function,/// which captures its name, and its argument names (thus implicitly the number/// of arguments the function takes), as well as if it is an operator.

Title: LLVM #6 — Compiling to Object Code. - DEV Community
URL: https://dev.to/lahari_tenneti_4a8a082e9c/llvm-6-compiling-to-object-code-1a47
Content: What I understood: The process of emitting object code includes picking a target, describing the machine, configuring the module, and emitting.
